mnemonics.title: PCA Steps
mnemonics.content: Standardize data first (S), Compute covariance matrix (C), Find eigenvectors/eigenvalues (E), Choose top k components (C), Transform data (T). SCECT remembers: Standardize, Covariance, Eigen, Choose, Transform.

mnemonics.title: Eigenvalue Meaning
mnemonics.content: Eigenvalue = 'Eigen' means 'own/characteristic' - eigenvalues represent the 'characteristic' importance (variance) of each principal component. Bigger is better!

mnemonics.title: Principal Component Order
mnemonics.content: PC1 = Most Variance (1st), PC2 = 2nd Most Variance... Think of 1st class seats = best view (most variance captured).

acronyms.usage: Dimensionality reduction technique transforming correlated features into uncorrelated principal components
Numerical method used to compute PCA more stably than eigen decomposition
Supervised dimensionality reduction maximizing class separability
Nonlinear extension using kernel functions for curved manifold data
Preserves pairwise distances during dimensionality reduction

visualTips.visualization: Draw a scree plot - bar chart with eigenvalue on Y-axis and component number on X-axis. Look for 'elbow' where curve bends. After elbow, adding more components gives diminishing returns.
Imagine rotating coordinate system to align with data's natural spread. First axis (PC1) points in direction of most stretching, second axis (PC2) perpendicular to first, and so on.
Think of projecting 3D data onto 2D plane - you're losing one dimension but trying to keep the 'shadow' (variance) as large as possible. The best angle shows the biggest shadow.

analogies.analogy: Like finding the best angles to view a 3D sculpture. First angle shows most detail, second angle (perpendicular) shows new details not visible from first angle.
Like taking a high-resolution photo and compressing it to lower resolution - you lose some detail but keep the essential information while saving space.
Like grades in different subjects - higher eigenvalue (grade) means that principal component (subject) is more important for describing overall performance (variance).
Like North-South and East-West directions - they are perpendicular (independent). Knowing how far north you are doesn't tell you anything about east-west position.
